name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَدْخُلُوا۟ بُيُوتًا غَيْرَ بُيُوتِكُمْ حَتَّىٰ تَسْتَأْنِسُوا۟ وَتُسَلِّمُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ (٢٧)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Do not enter houses other than your own until you have announced [your arrival] and greeted their occupants. That is better for you. Maybe you will take admonition. (27)
```

```arabic
فَإِن لَّمْ تَجِدُوا۟ فِيهَآ أَحَدًا فَلَا تَدْخُلُوهَا حَتَّىٰ يُؤْذَنَ لَكُمْ ۖ وَإِن قِيلَ لَكُمُ ٱرْجِعُوا۟ فَٱرْجِعُوا۟ ۖ هُوَ أَزْكَىٰ لَكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ عَلِيمٌ (٢٨)
```

```english
But if you do not find anyone in them, do not enter them until you are given permission. And if you are told: ‘Turn back,’ then do turn back. That is more decent for you. Allah knows best what you do. (28)
```

```arabic
لَّيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَن تَدْخُلُوا۟ بُيُوتًا غَيْرَ مَسْكُونَةٍ فِيهَا مَتَـٰعٌ لَّكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا تُبْدُونَ وَمَا تَكْتُمُونَ (٢٩)
```

```english
There will be no sin upon you in entering [without announcing] uninhabited houses wherein you have goods belonging to you. Allah knows whatever you disclose and whatever you conceal. (29)
```

```arabic
قُل لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَغُضُّوا۟ مِنْ أَبْصَـٰرِهِمْ وَيَحْفَظُوا۟ فُرُوجَهُمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ أَزْكَىٰ لَهُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا يَصْنَعُونَ (٣٠)
```

```english
Tell the faithful men to cast down their looks and to guard their private parts. That is more decent for them. Allah is indeed well aware of what they do. (30)
```

```arabic
وَقُل لِّلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ يَغْضُضْنَ مِنْ أَبْصَـٰرِهِنَّ وَيَحْفَظْنَ فُرُوجَهُنَّ وَلَا يُبْدِينَ زِينَتَهُنَّ إِلَّا مَا ظَهَرَ مِنْهَا ۖ وَلْيَضْرِبْنَ بِخُمُرِهِنَّ عَلَىٰ جُيُوبِهِنَّ ۖ وَلَا يُبْدِينَ زِينَتَهُنَّ إِلَّا لِبُعُولَتِهِنَّ أَوْ ءَابَآئِهِنَّ أَوْ ءَابَآءِ بُعُولَتِهِنَّ أَوْ أَبْنَآئِهِنَّ أَوْ أَبْنَآءِ بُعُولَتِهِنَّ أَوْ إِخْوَٰنِهِنَّ أَوْ بَنِىٓ إِخْوَٰنِهِنَّ أَوْ بَنِىٓ أَخَوَٰتِهِنَّ أَوْ نِسَآئِهِنَّ أَوْ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُنَّ أَوِ ٱلتَّـٰبِعِينَ غَيْرِ أُو۟لِى ٱلْإِرْبَةِ مِنَ ٱلرِّجَالِ أَوِ ٱلطِّفْلِ ٱلَّذِينَ لَمْ يَظْهَرُوا۟ عَلَىٰ عَوْرَٰتِ ٱلنِّسَآءِ ۖ وَلَا يَضْرِبْنَ بِأَرْجُلِهِنَّ لِيُعْلَمَ مَا يُخْفِينَ مِن زِينَتِهِنَّ ۚ وَتُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ جَمِيعًا أَيُّهَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (٣١)
```

```english
And tell the faithful women to cast down their looks and to guard their private parts, and not to display their charms, beyond what is [acceptably] visible, and let them draw their scarfs over their bosoms, and not display their charms except to their husbands, or their fathers, or their husband’s fathers, or their sons, or their husband’s sons, or their brothers, or their brothers’ sons, or their sisters’ sons, or their women, or their slave girls, or male dependants lacking [sexual] desire, or children who are not yet conscious of female sexuality. And let them not thump their feet to make known their hidden ornaments. Rally to Allah in repentance, O faithful, so that you may be felicitous. (31)
```

```arabic
وَأَنكِحُوا۟ ٱلْأَيَـٰمَىٰ مِنكُمْ وَٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ مِنْ عِبَادِكُمْ وَإِمَآئِكُمْ ۚ إِن يَكُونُوا۟ فُقَرَآءَ يُغْنِهِمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٣٢)
```

```english
Marry off those who are single among you, and the upright among your male and female slaves. If they are poor, Allah will enrich them out of His grace, and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing. (32)
```

```arabic
وَلْيَسْتَعْفِفِ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَجِدُونَ نِكَاحًا حَتَّىٰ يُغْنِيَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ ۗ وَٱلَّذِينَ يَبْتَغُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِمَّا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ فَكَاتِبُوهُمْ إِنْ عَلِمْتُمْ فِيهِمْ خَيْرًا ۖ وَءَاتُوهُم مِّن مَّالِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِىٓ ءَاتَىٰكُمْ ۚ وَلَا تُكْرِهُوا۟ فَتَيَـٰتِكُمْ عَلَى ٱلْبِغَآءِ إِنْ أَرَدْنَ تَحَصُّنًا لِّتَبْتَغُوا۟ عَرَضَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ وَمَن يُكْرِههُّنَّ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ إِكْرَٰهِهِنَّ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٣٣)
```

```english
Those who cannot afford marriage should be continent until Allah enriches them out of His grace. As for those who seek an emancipation deal from among your slaves, make such a deal with them if you know any good in them, and give them out of the wealth of Allah which He has given you. Do not compel your female slaves to prostitution when they desire to be chaste, seeking the transitory wares of the life of this world. Should anyone compel them, Allah will be forgiving and merciful to them following their compulsion. (33)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتٍ مُّبَيِّنَـٰتٍ وَمَثَلًا مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ خَلَوْا۟ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ وَمَوْعِظَةً لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ (٣٤)
```

```english
Certainly We have sent down to you manifest signs and a description of those who passed before you, and an advice for the Godwary. (34)
```

## (Commentary)

These are rulings and laws (أحكام وشرائع) that are proportionate and appropriate to what has preceded.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَدْخُلُوا بُيُوتاً غَيْرَ بُيُوتِكُمْ حَتَّى تَسْتَأْنِسُوا وَتُسَلِّمُوا عَلى أَهْلِها** — *O you who have faith! Do not enter houses other than your own until you have announced your presence and greeted their occupants.* (24:27) etc. Intimacy (الأنس) with a thing or toward it refers to familiarity and the heart’s tranquility toward it. Seeking intimacy (الاستيناس) is to seek that through an action that leads to it, such as seeking permission to enter a house by mentioning Allah, clearing one's throat, and the like, so that the owner of the house becomes aware that there is someone who wishes to enter upon him. Thus, he may prepare for that, as he might be in a state in which he does not wish anyone to see him or for an observer to look upon him.

From this, it becomes clear that the benefit (مصلحة) of this ruling is to veil the private affairs (عورات) of people and to safeguard the dignity of faith (كرامة الإيمان). When the one entering seeks intimacy upon wanting to enter a house other than his own, and informs the owner of the house of his seeking intimacy regarding his entry, and then enters and greets him, he has assisted him in veiling his private state and has granted him security from himself.

Persistence in this beautiful conduct leads to the strengthening of brotherhood, affection, and general cooperation in manifesting what is beautiful and veiling what is unseemly. To this, He refers by His saying: **ذلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ** — *That is better for you, so that you may take admonition.* (24:27). That is, perhaps by persisting in this conduct, you will remember what you must observe and revive of the tradition of brotherhood and the harmony of hearts, beneath which lies every social felicity.

It has been said that His saying: **لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ** (so that you may take admonition) is a justification for an omitted part, the estimation being: "You were told such-and-such so that you might remember the exhortations of Allah and act according to their requirements," and there is no harm in this interpretation.

It has also been said that in His saying: **حَتَّى تَسْتَأْنِسُوا وَتُسَلِّمُوا** (until you have announced your presence and greeted), there is a transposition (تقديم وتأخير), and the original order is "until you greet and announce your presence." This is as you see [unlikely].

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَإِنْ لَمْ تَجِدُوا فِيها أَحَداً فَلا تَدْخُلُوها حَتَّى يُؤْذَنَ لَكُمْ** — *And if you do not find anyone in them, do not enter them until permission is given to you.* (24:28) ... etc. That is, if you know that there is no one in them—meaning the one who possesses the right to grant permission—then do not enter them until permission is given to you by the one who possesses that right. The meaning is not that one should peek into the house and look inside, and if he sees no one, he refrains from entering; for the context (السياق) testifies that the prohibition is actually against looking and peering into the private affairs of people.

This verse clarifies the ruling on entering the house of another when there is no one inside who possesses the right to grant permission. The previous verse clarifies the ruling on entering when there is someone who possesses the right to grant permission and does not forbid it. As for entering when there is someone who possesses the right to grant permission but forbids it and does not allow entry, its ruling is clarified by His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنْ قِيلَ لَكُمُ ارْجِعُوا فَارْجِعُوا هُوَ أَزْكى لَكُمْ وَاللهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ عَلِيمٌ** — *And if it is said to you, ‘Turn back,’ then do turn back: that is more decent for you. And Allah knows best what you do.* (24:28).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُناحٌ أَنْ تَدْخُلُوا بُيُوتاً غَيْرَ مَسْكُونَةٍ فِيها مَتاعٌ لَكُمْ** — *There is no sin upon you in entering uninhabited houses wherein there is some utility for you.* (24:29) etc. The apparent context is that His saying: **فِيها مَتاعٌ لَكُمْ** (wherein there is some utility for you) is an adjective after an adjective for His saying: **بُيُوتاً** (houses), not a new sentence justifying His saying: **لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُناحٌ** (There is no sin upon you). It appears that utility (المتاع) here carries the meaning of benefit or enjoyment (الاستمتاع).

Thus, it contains the permissibility of entering houses prepared for various types of utility which are naturally uninhabited, such as inns (الخانات), public baths (الحمامات), mills (الأرحية), and the like; for their being designated for utility constitutes a general permission for entering them.

It has sometimes been said that what is meant by utility (المتاع) is the nominal meaning, which is furniture or goods placed for buying and selling, as in commercial houses and shops; for they are permitted for entry by a general permission. This is not without some distance [from the truth] due to the inadequacy of the wording to support it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَغُضُّوا مِنْ أَبْصارِهِمْ وَيَحْفَظُوا فُرُوجَهُمْ ذلِكَ أَزْكى لَهُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ خَبِيرٌ بِما يَصْنَعُونَ** — *Tell the faithful men to cast down their looks and to guard their private parts. That is more decent for them. Allah is indeed well aware of what they do.* (24:30). Lowering (الغض) is to bring the eyelid over the eyelid. Looks (الأبصار) is the plural of sight (بصر), which is the organ of vision. From here, it appears that the preposition "from" (مِنْ) in "from their looks" (مِنْ أَبْصارِهِمْ) is for the beginning of an end (ابتداء الغاية), not redundant, nor for genus, nor for partitivity (التبعيض), as various commentators have suggested. The meaning is that they should perform the act of lowering, originating from their looks.

As for His saying: **قُلْ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَغُضُّوا مِنْ أَبْصارِهِمْ**, since "they should lower" (يَغُضُّوا) is consequent upon His saying: "Tell" (قُلْ) in the manner of a response to a condition (جواب الشرط), this indicates that the "saying" (القول) carries the meaning of a command (الأمر). The meaning is: "Command them to lower their looks," and the estimation is: "Command them to lower [their looks], for if you command them to do so, they will lower [them]." The verse is a command to lower the gaze, or if you wish, say: a prohibition against looking at what is not permissible to look at, whether a male or female stranger, due to the absolute nature (الإطلاق) of the expression.

And His saying: **وَيَحْفَظُوا فُرُوجَهُمْ** (and to guard their private parts) means: "And command them to guard their private parts." The term *furjah* or *farj* (الفرج) refers to the gap or opening between two things, and it is used as a metonym (كنى به) for the private parts (السوأة). Upon this, the usage of the Quran—which is full of refinement and etiquette—has proceeded. Then its usage for this meaning became so frequent that it became like an explicit term (النص), as mentioned by al-Raghib.

The juxtaposition between His saying: **يَغُضُّوا مِنْ أَبْصارِهِمْ** (lower their looks) and **يَحْفَظُوا فُرُوجَهُمْ** (guard their private parts) suggests that what is meant by guarding the private parts is veiling them from being seen, not guarding them from adultery or sodomy as has been said. It has been narrated in a report from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Every verse in the Quran regarding the guarding of private parts refers to adultery—except for this verse, for it refers to looking."

Based on this, it is possible for the first of the two phrases to be restricted by the second, and the meaning of the verse would be the prohibition of looking at the private parts and the command to veil them.

Then He pointed to the aspect of benefit (المصلحة) in the ruling and urged them to be mindful regarding it by His saying: **ذلِكَ أَزْكى لَهُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ خَبِيرٌ بِما يَصْنَعُونَ** — *That is more decent for them. Allah is indeed well aware of what they do.* (24:30).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَقُلْ لِلْمُؤْمِناتِ يَغْضُضْنَ** — *And tell the faithful women to cast down their looks...* (24:31) etc. The discussion regarding His saying: **وَقُلْ لِلْمُؤْمِناتِ يَغْضُضْنَ مِنْ أَبْصارِهِنَّ وَيَحْفَظْنَ فُرُوجَهُنَ** is similar to what has passed regarding His saying: **قُلْ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَغُضُّوا مِنْ أَبْصارِهِمْ وَيَحْفَظُوا فُرُوجَهُمْ**. Thus, it is not permissible for them to look at what is not permissible to look at, and it is obligatory for them to veil their private parts from both male and female strangers.

As for His saying: **وَلا يُبْدِينَ زِينَتَهُنَّ إِلَّا ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها** — *and not to display their charms, except for what is outward* (24:31). Displaying (الإبداء) is to make manifest. What is meant by their charms (زينتهن) is the places of adornment, because the adornment itself, such as earrings or bracelets, is not prohibited from being displayed. Thus, what is meant by displaying the charms is displaying the places of those charms on the body.

Allah, the Glorified, has excepted from this "what is outward" (ما ظهر). Narrations have stated that what is meant by "what is outward" are the face, the hands, and the feet, as will come [later], if Allah wills.

And His saying: **وَلْيَضْرِبْنَ بِخُمُرِهِنَّ عَلى جُيُوبِهِنَ** — *and let them draw their scarfs over their bosoms* (24:31). *Khumur* (الخُمُر) is the plural of *khimar* (خمار), which is what a woman covers her head with and which drapes over her chest. *Juyub* (الجيوب) is the plural of *jayb* (جيب), which is well-known, and what is meant by *juyub* here are the bosoms (الصدور). The meaning is: let them cast the ends of their head-coverings over their bosoms to veil them.

And His saying: **وَلا يُبْدِينَ زِينَتَهُنَّ إِلَّا لِبُعُولَتِهِنَ** — *and not to display their charms except to their husbands* — until His saying — **أَوْ بَنِي أَخَواتِهِنَ** — *or their sisters’ sons* (24:31). The *bu'ulah* (البعولة) are their husbands. The other seven groups are their unmarriageable kin (محارمهن) through lineage or cause (النسب والسبب). The grandfathers of the husbands fall under the ruling of their fathers, and the grandsons of the husbands fall under the ruling of their sons.

And His saying: **أَوْ نِسائِهِنَ** — *or their women* (24:31). The possessive construction (الإضافة) indicates that what is meant by them are the believing women. Thus, it is not permissible for them to disrobe before other [non-believing] women, as has been mentioned in narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them).

And His saying: **أَوْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُهُنَ** — *or their slave-girls* (24:31). Its absolute nature includes both male and female slaves, and narrations have been reported to this effect, as will come, if Allah wills. This is one of the instances where "what" (ما) is used for rational beings (أولي العقل).

And His saying: **أَوِ التَّابِعِينَ غَيْرِ أُولِي الْإِرْبَةِ مِنَ الرِّجالِ** — *or male attendants who have no desire* (24:31). *Irba* (الإربة) is need, and what is meant here is the sexual desire (الشهوة) that necessitates marriage. "From the men" (مِنَ الرِّجالِ) is an explanation for the "attendants" (التابعين). What is meant by them, as explained by the narrations, are the simple-minded men (البله) who are under the guardianship of others and possess no sexual desire.

And His saying: **أَوِ الطِّفْلِ الَّذِينَ لَمْ يَظْهَرُوا عَلى عَوْراتِ النِّساءِ** — *or children who are not yet aware of women’s private parts* (24:31). That is, the group of children—the definite article "the" (اللام) being for exhaustiveness (الاستغراق)—who have not gained strength or "prevailed" (يظهروا)—from *zuhur* (الظهور) meaning dominance—over matters concerning women that are unseemly to mention explicitly. This is—as has been said—a metonym for puberty (البلوغ).

And His saying: **وَلا يَضْرِبْنَ بِأَرْجُلِهِنَّ لِيُعْلَمَ ما يُخْفِينَ مِنْ زِينَتِهِنَ** — *and let them not thump their feet to make known their hidden ornaments* (24:31). This occurs through the sound made by items of adornment such as anklets, necklaces, earrings, and bracelets.

And His saying: **وَتُوبُوا إِلَى اللهِ جَمِيعاً أَيُّهَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ** — *and rally to Allah in repentance, O faithful, so that you may be felicitous* (24:31). What is meant by repentance (التوبة)—according to what the context provides—is returning to Him, the Exalted, by complying with His commands and refraining from His prohibitions; in short, following His path.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَنْكِحُوا الْأَيامى مِنْكُمْ وَالصَّالِحِينَ مِنْ عِبادِكُمْ وَإِمائِكُمْ** — *Marry off those who are single among you, and the upright among your male slaves and your female slaves.* (24:32). *Inkah* (الإنكاح) is to marry off. *Ayama* (الأيامى) is the plural of *ayyim* (أيم), which refers to a male who has no female with him, or a female who has no male with her. Sometimes a woman is called *ayyimah*. What is meant by "the upright" (الصالحين) are those who are fit for marriage, not those who are righteous in their deeds.

And His saying: **إِنْ يَكُونُوا فُقَراءَ يُغْنِهِمُ اللهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ** — *If they are poor, Allah will enrich them out of His grace* (24:32). This is a beautiful promise of enrichment and abundance of provision (سعة الرزق), which He emphasized by His saying: **وَاللهُ واسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ** — *and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing.* (24:32). Provision (الرزق) follows the suitability of the one provided for, by the will of Allah, the Glorified. There will come to you, if Allah wills, in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **فَوَ رَبِّ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِنَّهُ لَحَقٌّ مِثْلَ ما أَنَّكُمْ تَنْطِقُونَ** — *By the Lord of the heaven and the earth, it is indeed as real as your speaking [to one another]!* (51:23), a discussion on the meaning of the abundance of provision.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلْيَسْتَعْفِفِ الَّذِينَ لا يَجِدُونَ نِكاحاً حَتَّى يُغْنِيَهُمُ اللهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ** — *Those who cannot afford marriage should be continent until Allah enriches them out of His grace.* (24:33). *Isti'faf* (الاستعفاف) and *ta'affuf* (التعفف) are close in meaning. What is meant by "not finding marriage" (عدم وجدان النكاح) is the lack of ability to provide the dower (المهر) and maintenance (النفقة). The meaning of the verse is a command to be continent for the one who is unable to marry, and to guard against falling into adultery until Allah enriches him from His grace.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالَّذِينَ يَبْتَغُونَ الْكِتابَ مِمَّا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ فَكاتِبُوهُمْ إِنْ عَلِمْتُمْ فِيهِمْ خَيْراً** — *As for those of your slaves who seek a contract [of manumission], make the contract with them if you know any good in them* (24:33) etc. What is meant by "the contract" (الكتاب) is the *mukatabah* (المكاتبة) — *the written agreement of manumission*. Seeking the contract is for the slave to ask his master to enter into a *mukatabah* agreement with him, whereby he pays the master a sum of money in exchange for his freedom. In the verse is a command to the masters to respond to them if they know "good" (خيرا) in them, which is a metonym for ascertaining their suitability for that.

And His saying: **وَآتُوهُمْ مِنْ مالِ اللهِ الَّذِي آتاكُمْ** — *and give them out of the wealth of Allah which He has given you* (24:33). This is an indication to give them the wealth for the *mukatabah* from the obligatory zakat (الزكاة المفروضة), as a portion of the zakat is designated for them, as He, the Exalted, said: **وَفي الرِّقابِ** — *and for [the freeing of] slaves* (9:60), or it refers to waiving a portion of the *mukatabah* payment.

In this verse and the preceding verses are numerous jurisprudential (فقهية) discussions that should be referred to in the books of jurisprudence.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلا تُكْرِهُوا فَتَياتِكُمْ عَلَى الْبِغاءِ إِنْ أَرَدْنَ تَحَصُّناً** — *Do not compel your slave-girls to prostitution if they intend to be chaste* (24:33). *Fatayat* (الفتيات) are female slaves and maidservants. *Bigha'* (البغاء) is adultery (الزنا), and it is on the form of *mufa'alah* from *baghy*. *Tahassun* (التحصن) is continence and marriage. "Seeking the transient goods of the life of this world" (وابتغاء عرض الحياة الدنيا) is the seeking of money. The meaning is clear.

The prohibition of compulsion was conditioned upon the "intention to be chaste" (إرادة التحصن) because compulsion (الإكراه) cannot be realized in one who does not desire chastity. Then He promised them forgiveness in the event of compulsion by His saying: **وَمَنْ يُكْرِهْهُنَّ فَإِنَّ اللهَ مِنْ بَعْدِ إِكْراهِهِنَّ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ** — *and should anyone compel them, then after their compulsion Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful.* (24:33). Its meaning is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكُمْ آياتٍ مُبَيِّناتٍ وَمَثَلاً مِنَ الَّذِينَ خَلَوْا مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ وَمَوْعِظَةً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ** — *Certainly We have sent down to you manifest signs and an example of those who passed away before you and an advice for the God-conscious.* (24:34). *Mathal* (المثل) is the description (الصفة). It is possible that His saying: **وَلَقَدْ أَنْزَلْنا** etc., is a circumstantial clause (حال) for the subject of His saying: **تُوبُوا** (repent) in the previous verse, or it is a new sentence (استيناف). The meaning is: "I swear, We have sent down to you verses that clarify for you the knowledge of religion (معارف الدين) through which you will be felicitous, and a description of those who preceded—their righteous and their wicked—by which it becomes distinct to you what you should adopt and what you should avoid, and an advice for the God-conscious (المتقين) among you."

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, with its chain of narration to Abd al-Rahman ibn Abi Abdillah, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **لا تَدْخُلُوا بُيُوتاً غَيْرَ بُيُوتِكُمْ ـ حَتَّى تَسْتَأْنِسُوا وَتُسَلِّمُوا عَلى أَهْلِها** — "He said: Seeking intimacy (الاستيناس) is the sound of the sandal and the greeting (التسليم)."

**I say:** It was also narrated by al-Saduq in *Ma'ani al-Akhbar* from Muhammad ibn al-Hasan in a *marfu'* report from Abd al-Rahman from him (peace be upon him).

In *al-Majma'*, from Abu Ayyub al-Ansari, who said: "We said: 'O Messenger of Allah, what is seeking intimacy (الاستيناس)?' He said: 'A man speaks by glorifying Allah (التسبيحة), praising Him (التحميدة), and magnifying Him (التكبيرة)—and he clears his throat for the occupants of the house.'"

From Sahl ibn Sa'd, who said: "A man peeked into one of the rooms of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) while the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had a comb (مدرى) with which he was scratching his head. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'If I had known you were looking, I would have poked your eyes with this—seeking permission (الاستيذان) is only because of looking.'"

It is narrated that a man said to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "Should I seek permission to enter upon my mother?" He said: "Yes." The man said: "She has no servant other than me—should I seek permission every time I enter?" He said: "Would you like to see her naked?" The man said: "No." He said: "Then seek permission to enter upon her."

It is narrated that a man sought permission to enter upon the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and cleared his throat. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to a woman called Rawdah: "Go to this man and teach him, and tell him: 'Say: Peace be upon you, may I enter?'" The man heard her and said it, so the Prophet said: "Enter."

**I say:** It is narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from a group of compilers of traditions: the first narration from Abu Ayyub, the second from Sahl ibn Sa'd, and the fourth from Amr ibn Sa'd al-Thaqafi.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ubadah ibn al-Samit: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was asked about seeking permission in houses, and he said: 'Whoever's eye enters before he seeks permission and greets—he has indeed disobeyed Allah and there is no permission for him.'"

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **فَإِنْ لَمْ تَجِدُوا فِيها أَحَداً ـ فَلا تَدْخُلُوها حَتَّى يُؤْذَنَ لَكُمْ**, he said: "Its meaning is: if you do not find anyone in it to give you permission—do not enter it until you are given permission."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُناحٌ أَنْ تَدْخُلُوا ـ بُيُوتاً غَيْرَ مَسْكُونَةٍ فِيها مَتاعٌ لَكُمْ**, Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: "They are the public baths, the inns, and the mills; you enter them without permission."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration to Abu Amr al-Zubayri, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), in a hadith mentioning what Allah has made obligatory upon the limbs, he said: "**وفرض على البصر أن لا ينظر إلى ما حرم الله عليه ، وأن يعرض عما نهى الله عنه مما لا يحل له ـ وهو عمله وهو من الإيمان. فقال تبارك وتعالى : «قُلْ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَغُضُّوا مِنْ أَبْصارِهِمْ وَيَحْفَظُوا فُرُوجَهُمْ» فنهاهم أن ينظروا إلى عوراتهم ـ وأن ينظر المرء إلى فرج أخيه ـ ويحفظ فرجه أن ينظر إليه ، وقال : «وَقُلْ لِلْمُؤْمِناتِ يَغْضُضْنَ مِنْ أَبْصارِهِنَّ وَيَحْفَظْنَ فُرُوجَهُنَ» من أن تنظر إحداهن إلى فرج أختها ـ وتحفظ فرجها من أن ينظر إليه. وقال : كل شيء في القرآن من حفظ الفرج فهو من الزنا ـ إلا هذه الآية فهو من النظر.**" — "And He made it obligatory upon the sight that it should not look at what Allah has forbidden to it, and that it should turn away from what Allah has prohibited of that which is not permissible for it—and this is its action and it is part of faith. Thus, He, the Blessed and Exalted, said: 'Tell the faithful men to cast down their looks and to guard their private parts.' So He forbade them from looking at their private parts—and that a man should look at the private part of his brother—and that he should guard his private part from being looked at. And He said: 'And tell the faithful women to cast down their looks and to guard their private parts'—from one of them looking at the private part of her sister—and that she should guard her private part from being looked at. And He said: 'Every instance in the Quran regarding the guarding of the private part refers to adultery—except for this verse, for it refers to looking.'"

**I say:** Al-Qummi narrated in his *Tafsir*, following the hadith, from his father, from Ibn Abi Umayr, from Abu Basir, from him (peace be upon him). Similar reports were narrated from Abu al-Aliyah and Ibn Zayd.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration to Sa'd al-Iskaf, from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him), who said: "A young man from the Ansar met a woman in Medina—and women used to wear their head-coverings behind their ears—so he looked at her as she approached. When she passed, he looked at her and entered an alleyway which he named as belonging to the sons of so-and-so, and he began looking behind her. A bone in the wall or a piece of glass struck his face and cut it. When the woman had passed, he looked and saw blood flowing onto his garment and chest. He said: 'By Allah, I shall surely go to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and inform him.' He said: So he came to him, and when the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saw him, he said: 'What is this?' So he informed him, and Gabriel descended with this verse: **قُلْ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَغُضُّوا مِنْ أَبْصارِهِمْ وَيَحْفَظُوا فُرُوجَهُمْ ـ ذلِكَ أَزْكى لَهُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ خَبِيرٌ بِما يَصْنَعُونَ**."

**I say:** It was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Mardawayh from Ali ibn Abi Talib in a similar fashion. The apparent meaning of the hadith is that what is meant by the command to lower the gaze in the verse is the prohibition of looking at a female stranger in general, just as the apparent meaning of some previous narrations is that it is a prohibition against looking at the private parts of another specifically.

In the same source, with its chain of narration to Marwak ibn Ubayd, from some of our companions, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "I said to him: 'What is permissible to be seen of a woman when one is not a mahram?' He said: 'The face, the two hands, and the two feet.'"

**I say:** It was narrated in *al-Khisal* from some of our companions from him (peace be upon him), and its wording is: "The face, the two hands, and the two feet."

In *Qurb al-Isnad* by al-Himyari, from Ali ibn Ja'far, from his brother Musa ibn Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "I asked him about a man: what is proper for him to look at of a woman who is not permissible for him? He said: 'The face, the hand, and the place of the bracelet.'"

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration to Abbad ibn Suhayb, who said: "I heard Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) say: '**لا بأس بالنظر إلى رءوس أهل تهامة والأعراب ـ وأهل السواد والعلوج ـ لأنهم إذا نهوا لا ينتهون. قال : والمجنونة والمغلوبة على عقلها ، ولا بأس بالنظر إلى شعرها وجسدها ما لم يتعمد ذلك.**'" — "There is no harm in looking at the heads of the people of Tihamah, the Bedouins, the people of the Sawad, and the non-Arabs (العلوج)—because when they are forbidden, they do not desist. He said: And the insane woman and the one whose intellect is overcome; there is no harm in looking at her hair and her body as long as it is not done intentionally."

**I say:** It is as if he (peace be upon him) means by his saying "as long as it is not done intentionally" the presence of suspicious desire (الريبة).

In *al-Khisal*, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): "**يا علي أول نظرة لك والثانية عليك لا لك**" — "O Ali, the first look is for you, and the second is against you, not for you."

**I say:** A similar report was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from several compilers of traditions from Buraydah from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and its wording is: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to Ali: 'Do not follow a look with another look—for the first is for you, but the latter is not for you.'"

In *Jawami' al-Jami'*, from Umm Salamah, who said: "I was with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and Maymunah was with him, when Ibn Umm Maktum approached—and that was after we had been commanded to wear the hijab. He said: 'Observe the hijab.' We said: 'O Messenger of Allah, is he not blind and cannot see us?' He said: 'Are you two blind? Do you not see him?'"

**I say:** It was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, and al-Bayhaqi from her.

In *al-Faqih*, Hafs ibn al-Bakhtari narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "It is not proper for a woman to uncover herself before a Jewish or Christian woman, for they describe that to their husbands."

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **أَوْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُهُنَ**, it was said: "Its meaning is male and female slaves," and that was narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him).

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration to Abd al-Rahman ibn Abi Abdillah, who said: "I asked him about 'male attendants who have no desire.' He said: 'The simpleton who is under guardianship and does not approach women.'"

In the same source, with its chain of narration to Muhammad ibn Ja'far, from his father, from his ancestors (peace be upon them), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: '**من ترك التزويج مخافة العيلة ـ فقد أساء ظنه بالله عز وجل إن الله يقول «إِنْ يَكُونُوا فُقَراءَ يُغْنِهِمُ اللهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ»**'" — "Whoever abandons marriage for fear of poverty has indeed thought ill of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic; for Allah says: 'If they are poor, Allah will enrich them out of His grace.'"

**I say:** Regarding the previous meanings, there are very many narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them); whoever desires them should refer to the books of hadith.

In *al-Faqih*, al-Ala' narrated from Muhammad ibn Muslim from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **فَكاتِبُوهُمْ إِنْ عَلِمْتُمْ فِيهِمْ خَيْراً** — "He said: The 'good' (الخير) is that he bears witness that there is no god but Allah and that Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah, and that he has a work in his hand by which he can earn, or that he has a craft."

**I say:** There are other narrations with this meaning.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration to al-Ala' ibn Fudayl from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **فَكاتِبُوهُمْ إِنْ عَلِمْتُمْ فِيهِمْ خَيْراً ـ وَآتُوهُمْ مِنْ مالِ اللهِ الَّذِي آتاكُمْ** — "He said: You should waive for him some of his installments (نجومه) that you did not intend to reduce, and do not exceed what is in your soul. I said: 'How much?' He said: 'Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) waived one thousand out of six thousand for a slave.'"

**I say:** It was narrated in *Majma' al-Bayan*, and likewise in *al-Durr al-Manthur*, from Ali (peace be upon him): "one-fourth of the wealth." What is understood from the apparent meanings of the reports is that no specific proportional amount is fixed.

The narration of al-Ayyashi has already preceded under the saying: **وَفي الرِّقابِ** — *and for [the freeing of] slaves* (9:60) in the ninth volume of this book, stating that the *mukatab* is given from the portion of the zakat designated for slaves.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَلا تُكْرِهُوا فَتَياتِكُمْ عَلَى الْبِغاءِ إِنْ أَرَدْنَ تَحَصُّناً**, he said: "The Arabs and Quraysh used to buy slave-girls and impose a heavy tax upon them, saying: 'Go and commit adultery and earn.' So Allah forbade them from that and said: 'Do not compel your slave-girls to prostitution'—until His saying—'all-forgiving, all-merciful,' meaning that Allah, the Exalted, will not take them to task for that if they were compelled to it."

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **لِتَبْتَغُوا عَرَضَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا**, it was said: "Abdullah ibn Ubayy had six slave-girls whom he used to compel to earn through adultery. When the prohibition of adultery was revealed, they came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and complained to him, so the verse was revealed."

**I say:** As for his having slave-girls whom he compelled to adultery, narrations have been reported to this effect in *al-Durr al-Manthur*, just as it narrated this report. As for this being after the revelation of the prohibition of adultery, it is weakened by the fact that adultery was not prohibited in Medina, but rather in Mecca before the Hijrah; indeed, its prohibition was among the essentials of Islam since the true call appeared. It has already preceded in the commentary on Surah al-An'am that the prohibition of indecencies (الفواحش), including adultery, is among the general rulings that are not specific to one law (شريعة) over another.
